input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue with keyboard handling in .NET 10. This is a known change — `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated/removed in Android 11+ and .NET MAUI has been evolving its approach. Let me provide guidance based on what I know.

In .NET 10 (and even late .NET 9), the recommended approach for Android is to use the `Edge-to-Edge` display model and handle keyboard insets properly. The `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated in Android API 30+ in favor of `WindowInsets` APIs.


For .NET MAUI specifically, I should clarify the current best practices: the older `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` approach has given way to using `WindowCompat.SetDecorFitsSystemWindows` with insets listeners from AndroidX.Core, or alternatively setting `android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize"` directly in the manifest, though that's also deprecated at the Android framework level.

The most practical solutions in .NET MAUI are either configuring the soft input mode in `MainActivity.cs`, adding the manifest attribute, or leveraging the `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` feature available in .NET 8+. Since the user mentioned `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` isn't working for them, I should explore what's actually preventing it from functioning in their setup. Setting up the Android platform code...

For iOS, the keyboard handling works differently and usually doesn't require as much manual intervention, though enabling `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` can help in some cases. The core issue with .NET 10 is that edge-to-edge rendering is now the default on Android, so I need to properly manage the IME insets to prevent the keyboard from overlapping content.
